speaker: interviewer
text: What was it like to arrive in this country and be in Bangor, Maine?

speaker: interviewee
text: Absolutely-- we arrived on Mother's Day. And it was absolutely unbelievable. Just absolutely. I thought, my God, there's still kind people in this world left. My aunt met me by the boat with a corsage. Never knew what Mother's Day was. I didn't know. I was overwhelmed. And, of course, we took the plane ride to Bangor. And the people were so kind, so warm. You know, not speaking the language, you find-- absolutely fantastic.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a story running in the newspapers about us. And people started-- my-- you know, it was-- Bangor is also a small community, and the place that they found us to live were very, very kind people in Bangor. [? And ?] they knew that the refugees that came over that-- where they're staying, so they would-- they had a grocery store, so they would come to her and say, can we bring her this? And can we bring her that?

speaker: interviewee
text: But I was an extremely, but a little bit too extremely proud person. Never wanted anything. I told my husband-- my child was three years old. I said, you must never accept anything from anybody. I don't want people should feel sorry for us. Because I know we're young. We're going to make it. I don't have a proper coat. I don't-- I'm not dressed properly. Neither is my child. Yeah, my-- for him, I made clothes for him. He was three years old, so I was able to make him clothes. I knew how to knit and how to crochet. And I made him clothes, so he was dressed marvelously. We were not dressed, but I told her, I says, absolutely nothing. I will not accept anything from anybody. One day a lady came with a fur coat. And she says, I see what you're wearing. Take this coat. You'll make me very happy. I said, then come to the services at night. I took the coat. I was sleeping on it until the next day, and then I said to my landlady, please, you call her and tell her to come and take the coat back. I just don't want the coat. I don't feel comfortable, and I don't want anybody should give me anything. People would bring toys for the child. And I would say, you have to return it. I was too extreme, but I am still glad I have never-- I have always said to my children, I was stripped of everything in life. My self-respect is the only thing that I have left and my pride. Never taken a nickel. First the Joint came, and they says they came to pay the rent for one month, and they're going to bring me the monthly rent every month. So I cried hysterically. I said, I don't-- I have enough to pay the rent. I don't want the rent. But she says, it is not us. It is-- we wanted-- this is a gift for you. I says, but I don't want it. But they insisted. And that one month rent was paid. Two weeks later, my husband had a job. And he worked. Never, never did we take anything from them. We had very rough times, but never taken anything from anybody. And I always used to say, this is how I want it.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you live in Maine?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Maine, we lived six years. And I wanted to give my children more of a Jewish education, and Bangor didn't have it. And, also, I maybe would have stayed a little longer, but my landlord died. And I was extremely attached to him. Like my child called her father Zadie, her mother Bubbie. And they-- she said-- my landlady-- she said she is leaving the city. She cannot stay any longer there. So I says, it's time for us to go, also.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now was your daughter born there?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Yes. Excuse me. Yes, my daughter was born in 1951.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her name is?

speaker: interviewee
text: Betty. Yeah, she was born there. And she was five years old when she left. But there was always-- between the children, we all went back to see old friends and always a very, very warm feeling. We came to New York in '56. And in New York, we had a-- my husband had a very, very rough time finding a job. When he found a job, it was only a part time job with two fares to travel in New York and every penny counted so terribly. But he did take it. He walked a lot. It kept him in shape. And then till-- from '56-- six years, also-- in 19-- New York was very rough on me. I-- coming from a small community, and I prefer the smaller places. I had a hard time to get adjusted to New York-- very hard time. The pushing on the subways and all that. I-- very hard time. I was very unhappy in New York. And my husband had an uncle here in Pasadena, and he had a heart condition. He was in business in the art business, also-- wholesale art. So he came, and he talked to my husband. He should come out, and he should work for him.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he came out here, and my husband stayed here a year without the family to see whether he liked it or he's going to make a go of it. And he loved it, and he did very, very well. But it didn't click with his uncle, because it just didn't work out.

speaker: interviewee
text: His uncle knocked him a great deal. Called him greenhorn and doesn't even speak the proper English and all this, and that hurt terribly. Hurt us terribly, because he was a very ambitious person. And he always made a good living. But here he knocked him, so after 1962-- in the 1960s-- '62-- the end of 1962, he went on his own.

speaker: interviewer
text: And is that when you moved here?

speaker: interviewee
text: Excuse me?

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you move to California?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1962. He came back to New York. We sold-- we had already a house there. We sold it and moved out here. And my sister came out for vacation here the first year that we-- and she didn't go back. She stayed on. I had nobody here. Absolutely nobody. So we encouraged her, and they went in business together-- her husband. And thank God, they were very successful. Made a good liv-- very, very hard workers. My husband used to-- the salesman for the company-- travelled a great deal. And I was working with him in the office. I had taken courses at night school-- typing and bookkeeping. And so I was the secretary, bookkeeper. And we made of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the children went to school?

speaker: interviewee
text: My children went to school. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Betty and David-- they both graduated Beverly High School. They we-- both went to Berkeley. And then David went to law school here in SC. And things were going wonderfully. David found a girl that I loved. Wonderful, wonderful family. Married her. And the only thing is the tragic thing that happened is that when he-- after he took the bar, he went-- he never had any vacations. And here he was married. He didn't take orders from his parents, because we were strict disciplinarians. I have to say that. We're European, and I always used to say we are Europeans. I don't care who says what. But you're going to do it the way we think is best.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he was-- really never been away from home. So they went to Europe. He bought a small Fiat in Europe, and they were travelling. And they came to a-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Athens. They were going to Athens to put the car on the boat and go to Israel for the first time.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he had a terrible-- it was raining, and he was going through the mountains. And he wasn't-- he wasn't used to traveling. He was a California driver. He wasn't used to traveling in narrow, wet, hilly roads. He had a terrible accident. And make a long story short, with all-- whatever we tried from here to there, she was paralyzed from--

speaker: interviewer
text: And what's her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name is Jackie. My favorite daughter-in-law. We always call her our favorite daughter-in-law.

speaker: interviewer
text: But still they've had children, right?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, she had-- thank God-- six children. But it was-- it's very hard, because she's a very, very, very-- have to stress-- brave girl. Exceptional kid in every way. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I still break down because of it. [CRYING] That was the second Holocaust for me. I couldn't-- beautiful, beautiful girl. Very-- but I think she's making it. She made the best of it. Never complains, even she's in pain. She has a very bad back. Never complains. Thank God, she had six children. One little girl, Sarah, didn't make it. She passed away at nine months. But the others are wonderful. The oldest is Adam-- the love of my life is the first grandchild.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was he named for?

speaker: interviewee
text: It actually-- she wanted to name him after a long life. So she named him Adam. He has, also, my brother's middle name. And also at that time, in 1973, the Israeli War woke up, and my husband's sister's son-- a young age, brilliant guy-- was killed in the war. So Simcha.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then it's Zvi, which is a jolly little fellow. He just made the-- when was it? Three years ago, he made a movie. By coincidence, they picked him. And this is what he wants to go in. He did.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. You might have seen it. it was called. It's been on television a few times already. And then there is Shaya He's 15. And then there's the two girls, Aden and Branna. And my daughter also married a very nice boy. Wonderful. I'm very exceptionally lucky that I have a wonderful daughter-in-law and a very fine son-in-law. Couldn't have picked a better one.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name is Herb [? Meyers. ?] He's an attorney.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she has children?

speaker: interviewee
text: And she has six children. Wonderful children. And they give an awful lot-- both of them give an awful lots of themselves to the children, and it reflects. The children are all excellent students. And many times, I said, I should have never survived. But I look at the children. I say, it was worthwhile.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you think you were able to survive?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not by willpower. I must say that. Not by willpower. Sheer coincidence. Just really sheer coincidences, not willpower. Because the willpower wasn't there. I can't really-- many, many times maybe it was very, very hard on my children, also, even though I have never talked about my background to my children, because it was very painful.

speaker: interviewee
text: But nevertheless, any little thing that upset me, I had the terrible expression of saying, for this I had to survive? And then I would-- I would kick myself for it. I was aware of it that that's very rough on the kids. But the next time it came along, I did the same thing.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's always-- our-- the survivors that lived through what we all lived through-- we all lived through the same thing-- really needed guidance very, very much. And we never had it. Look, it comes on real quick. Thank God for this wonderful, wonderful country and only I think people like us appreciate it more than those that were really born-- they cannot feel the way we feel, to be grateful to this country that it's freedom, number one, which unless you experienced the horrors, you know what freedom means. It comes on real quick. It's right there, guiding the children, talking to the children, explaining to the children. And there's always that help available today for anybody, whether you're capable financially or you're not capable, it is there if you want it. For us, there was nothing. And that terrible burden-- you carried it in your heart and on your shoulders. I know I couldn't get-- I couldn't get a job. I tried in New York very hard. Because I had no experience in anything, I couldn't get a job. So I was lucky in Saks. Before Christmas, they hired me. And I felt, my God, they hired me. They hired me-- Christmas.

speaker: interviewee
text: So when the guy was interviewing me, he said to me-- why are you so sad? Why are you so tense? He says, you're hired. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] He says, think more of yourself. You're going to be capable of doing it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, after I was hired for the Christmas season, I was the only one that-- the buyer asked me if I would stay on. And I stayed on, and she told me-- and I told her I don't work on Fridays. And she said, that's OK. And she asked me if she would-- if I would consider staying with Saks, and she would train me as an assistant buyer. She said, people like you. I was never in the group of talking or gossiping. I took my work very seriously, because it was such a big thing. They hired me. I am able to buy a coffee for my own money. And this is what I always used to say. I have to prove to myself that I can do something, also. This is why to me those things are terribly appreciated.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we never had the guidance. My husband used to say many times-- it took him a long time. It took him over a year. That I would sigh. He said, why are you sighing always? I says, I'm not aware of it even-- I'm sighing.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I was sighing. Even in my sleep, I was sighing. So he says, you're not yourself. You don't think much of yourself. And this is-- you know, in your mind you were-- it's like an orphan that has nobody left, but comes from a home that the parents passed away, and they put in a-- just thrown out without any guidance or anything.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is how I felt in my heart-- that any little thing that was done for me-- and I don't mean materially or financially or anything-- a kind word meant so much to me. They're nice to me. I wondered why. Why are they nice to me? Until it really-- in Bangor, the people have proven to me that they are just kind, goodhearted people. They're all I needed.

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister even say, which-- as I said, she was stronger than I am-- I always looked for a mother image. I missed that terribly. I was terribly attached to my mother, even though I was the oldest. But you miss that in life. But we had a wonderful life here in this country. Thank God my children are doing well, except for my daughter-in-law. When I see her, my heart aches. Still aches. And she never complains, because she knows I take it very seriously. My relationship with my daughter-in-law is, if not better, as close as to my daughter. If not better. She has more time, so she will-- if I don't sound right, she'll call three times a day. How I feel. Never misses a day. And I really have a lot-- an awful lot to be grateful to.

speaker: interviewer
text: If you had to say something as a message to the people who will be watching this in the future, what would it be?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would say you must always be involved. Don't ignore it by thinking, well, this is the other guy, and I cannot be involved. I would say you have to be involved. Because if not, God forbid, the same thing can happen again. No matter how wonderful the country, it only need-- you only need a couple of people that starts spreading rumors. And the whole darn thing can come tumbling worse than an earthquake. This is all I would say. You must be involved. You must never-- hatred-- I have always-- my children never-- they should never possess any hatred, because hatred destroys. And I would say that among the worst, there were some good people still. Even among the Nazis, with all the horror stories, there was some goodness in them. Nobody is 100% one way or the other way. There is something-- in the best of us, something that we don't like about ourselves. And there is-- in the worst, there is some humanity in them still. And this would be my only messages. You must be involved.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Now you have some pictures you want to show.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. This is my family.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, thanks.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I--

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to--

speaker: interviewee
text: And also--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. First, before the pictures, you'd like to introduce your husband.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. This is my husband who we're going to be--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, Mollie, could you introduce us?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. This is my husband, Morris. And we have been married, and we're going to celebrate our 50th anniversary this year. And life has been-- can't complain-- pretty good. We had our little ups and downs. But life without that is no fun.

speaker: interviewer
text: Morris, where and when were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in Petrova, Romania. I lived all my life in Czechoslovakia.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so you two survived the Holocaust?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I was-- and I was in a labor camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in a labor camp for three years almost in the Hungarian-- and I was liberated in-- from the Russians in 1944 in November.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then you met Mollie and swept her off her feet?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I met her uncle. And I knew him before the war and then together. And I met him in a-- I went-- I went-- after the liberation, they wanted us to join in the army. And I just came home. I mean, tired and everything. And here they are, grabbing us to the army. I said, I'm-- I have another brother. And I'm looking for him, and we will join together. When I met a friend, he said, let's go to Romania. There I heard the Jews survived. From there, we-- from my hometown, I walked, and a little bit driving-- I mean, but that-- and we got to Romania-- to Bucharest after three weeks travelling. And there it was a few other boys who were liberated. And there I met her uncle. I mean, my-- he was a good friend. I know them. Then it turned out he was the uncle. And I was to got with him for a while. Then he went back to my-- to home to see about survivors. And after a few months, when everybody came home, then I find out. Then I was looking, too, for survivors. I don't find them.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then he asked me, what are you doing? He said, won't you come settle there to the [INAUDIBLE]? So I went there. There we met each other. Well, and now--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now we're going to see some pictures--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --of what this all has brought.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a photo of my father taken around 1928 and the same of my mother-- 1928.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were they taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were taken in Bilky, a photographer-- probably a local.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's name is Dawid, David. And my mother's is Rivka or Regina, they called her.

speaker: interviewer
text: And their last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father is Menglovitz. My mother's maiden name is Pascowitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Bilky-- and my father was from Bilky, Czechoslovakia, and my mother was a neighboring community, [NON-ENGLISH] Czechoslovakia.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the picture was taken in Bilky?

speaker: interviewee
text: The picture was taken approximately in 1928. This is a wedding picture of my husband and myself taken three years after we were married, because when I was married, there was no wedding gown. And I was-- wanted to have a wedding picture, so it was taken three years later, my sister's wedding.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Germany in Furth. This is my uncle, Stanley Pascowitz. He's my mother's brother. And he-- the photo was taken in Furth, approximately in 1946.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this is the uncle who--

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the uncle that picked up the remnants that the Nazis left.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where is Furth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Furth, Germany. Sorry. This is a photo of my sister, Rosalyn Preisler, and myself taken in Bangor, Maine, approximately 1952.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which is you, and which is her?

speaker: interviewee
text: The taller one is me. The smaller one is my sister, Rosalyn.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my son, David's family. Starting from the top right-- my oldest grandson Adam, the second one is Shaya, and the third one is Zvi. And the bottom is-- starting from the left is David holding his daughter Aden and Jackie holding her daughter Branna. And there is a picture of Sarah, which unfortunately didn't survive.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: The picture was taken in 1991.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: Here in Los Angeles, California. Just about Sarah?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Sarah passed away after seven months. This is my daughter, Betty Stauber and Herb Meyers at top. And the children from left to right is Ami, and Asher, Elan, and Mendel.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: These are the four children out of six. And it was taken in '93-- 1993 in Los Angeles.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: These two are Betty's two children. The left side is Michal, and on the right side is Dina-- my two grandchildren-- taken in Los Angeles in 1993 and in '94.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what are their last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Meyer. These are my son's two children. Aden on the left hand side and Branna on the right hand side. And this was taken in 1992.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Los Angeles, California.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, thank you. At what occasion?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was a bar mitzvah. The youngest son. It's Shaya's bar mitzvah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Thank you so much.
